medications.drug_name: central neuromodulator (e.g. low-dose amitriptyline, or an SNRI such as duloxetine, or a gabapentinoid such as pregabalin)
medications.indication_rationale: Central-pain modulation is the mainstay of fibromyalgia management — it 'turns down the volume' the standard analgesics cannot reach by dampening central signalling and augmenting descending inhibition. The source note's plan is to optimise chronic neuro-modulators at GP review; in telehealth the AI Doctor recommends and explains this rather than prescribing definitively, and no specific starting dose is synthesised (none is stated in the source).
contraindications_in_this_case: Agent-specific cautions apply and must be confirmed by the prescribing GP (e.g. TCA cautions: cardiac conduction disease/recent MI, narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention; gabapentinoid: sedation and misuse risk) — none documented here
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive CNS sedation with the single dose of diazepam given tonight — counsel about drowsiness and avoid other sedatives/alcohol

medications.indication_rationale: Named in the source note (celecoxib 200 mg daily) as part of the targeted strategy. A COX-2 NSAID has at best a limited adjunct role in nociplastic pain, which is centrally driven and analgesic-refractory; GP-initiated. Crucially, it must NOT be stacked on top of the maximum daily OTC NSAID already taken today — start no earlier than the next day within safe limits.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 200 mg orally once daily (as stated in the source note)
medications.duration: ongoing daily, reviewed by the GP
contraindications_in_this_case: Do NOT combine with the OTC NSAID already taken to the daily maximum today — additive GI and renal toxicity
Standard NSAID cautions: peptic ulcer/GI bleeding history, renal impairment, uncontrolled hypertension, cardiovascular disease — to be confirmed by the prescribing GP
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive NSAID toxicity if taken together with today's OTC anti-inflammatory — space dosing and stay within daily limits

medications.indication_rationale: A single low dose to break the acute paraspinal spasm and hypervigilance/panic cycle during this crisis, as given in the source note. Strictly one-off — not a treatment for fibromyalgia and not to be continued. In telehealth this is a clinician-prescribing decision, not something the AI Doctor administers.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5 mg orally, single dose once only (as stated in the source note)
medications.duration: single dose only — do not continue
contraindications_in_this_case: Not for ongoing use; dependence and sedation risk
Avoid combining with other CNS depressants or alcohol; caution given a concurrent central neuromodulator
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive sedation with a central neuromodulator — single dose, counsel about drowsiness
medications.deprescribing_note: Strictly a single dose to break the acute spasm/panic cycle. Do NOT start or continue a benzodiazepine course — no benefit in chronic centralised pain and a real dependence risk.

medications.indication_rationale: Named in the source note as part of the targeted strategy. Low-grade evidence in fibromyalgia; reasonable as a patient-preference adjunct but not a substitute for central-pain modulation. No dose stated in the note, so none is specified here.

Named in the source note as part of the targeted strategy. Off-label with limited/low-grade evidence in fibromyalgia and specialist/Authorised-Prescriber territory in Australia; not a first-line or telehealth-initiated agent. No dose stated in the note, so none is specified here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation and impairment risk, especially combined with diazepam and a central neuromodulator; specialist/Authorised-Prescriber pathway required

medications.drug_name: additional over-the-counter anti-inflammatory or paracetamol tonight
medications.indication_rationale: The patient has already taken the full daily allowance of OTC NSAIDs within a four-hour window. Further NSAID risks GI/renal harm and any further paracetamol risks hepatotoxicity; peripheral analgesics do not treat centralised pain in any case, and the source explicitly flags medication-error risk.
medications.deprescribing_note: No further anti-inflammatory painkillers (or paracetamol) tonight — the safe daily limit has been reached. Resume within normal daily limits only from tomorrow if used at all.

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are ineffective and harmful in fibromyalgia/nociplastic pain — they risk dependence, opioid-induced hyperalgesia (worsening central sensitisation) and sedation. Escalating to an opioid for this flare is a clear error of commission.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Trauma-informed chronic-pain psychology (CBT/ACT, pain-reprocessing, catastrophising and hypervigilance/anxiety management) is core treatment for fibromyalgia and directly addresses the post-traumatic distress driving this flare.
Trauma-informed / pain-informed physiotherapy with graded activity and pacing supports desensitisation and function in fibromyalgia. The source note refers to trauma-informed physical therapy.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Practise about 5 minutes of slow diaphragmatic breathing or progressive muscle relaxation before attempting to move from the bed, to down-regulate the over-active nervous system and reduce muscle guarding.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces sympathetic arousal and the central amplification of pain, and eases the guarding that makes movement agonising — a self-applied tool for tonight (mirrors the source's progressive-muscle-relaxation advice).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the room quiet and dimly lit tonight and reduce sudden sensory input; a weighted blanket can help with sensory grounding.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Lowering external sensory load prevents the over-active nervous system from finding new triggers and supports a faster return to baseline (mirrors the source's environment and weighted-blanket advice).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the crisis settles, pace activities (stop before the pain peaks) rather than boom-and-bust, and keep gently mobile within comfort.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Activity pacing prevents wind-up and the flare-crash cycle and supports gradual desensitisation.

patient_education_points.point: Your nervous system is amplifying normal signals and 'replaying the alarm' from your old injury — the volume is turned up too high. It does NOT mean new damage has happened to your body tonight.
These flares are intensely painful but physically safe, and they typically peak within 24-48 hours and then slowly settle as your nervous system recalibrates.

Standard painkillers fail because the problem is the nerve 'volume' being turned up, not new tissue damage — the ongoing nerve-calming medication your GP optimises works by turning that volume dial down.
Do NOT take any more anti-inflammatory painkillers or paracetamol tonight — you have already reached the safe daily limit and more could harm your stomach, kidneys or liver.
Keep your room quiet and dimly lit, use gentle relaxation and breathing before moving, and consider a weighted blanket for sensory grounding.

Safety net: if you lose control of your bladder or bowels, go numb around the groin or saddle area, or your legs suddenly give way — call 000 or go to an emergency department immediately. If your urine turns dark like tea or cola, or you're passing much less, get urgent medical review.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within about 3 days (sooner, within 24-48 hours, if distress persists or the patient cannot cope)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: pain and function, mood and distress and any self-harm risk, a formal neuromuscular examination, response to and tolerance of the central neuromodulator, and medication safety (OTC analgesic use)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: worsening or new neurological symptoms, any cauda-equina feature, dark urine or reduced output, escalating distress or thoughts of self-harm, or fever/systemic features

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of known post-traumatic central-sensitisation (fibromyalgia) pain, not a new structural, muscular or cardiovascular emergency
Perform a cauda-equina and neurological red-flag screen by history (bowel/bladder, saddle sensation, leg power/weakness)
Perform a rhabdomyolysis screen by history (dark tea/cola urine, reduced output, crush/immobility mechanism)
Reassure the patient it is physically safe and does not mean new damage — address the catastrophising and hypervigilance
Recommend central-pain modulation (e.g. low-dose tricyclic, SNRI or gabapentinoid) optimised via the GP
Advise NO further anti-inflammatory painkillers or paracetamol tonight (already at the safe daily maximum)
Offer non-drug measures — relaxation/breathing before moving, a quiet dimly-lit environment, sensory grounding
Arrange prompt GP review (about 3 days, sooner if not coping) with trauma-informed physiotherapy and pain-psychology referral
Give explicit cauda-equina and rhabdomyolysis safety nets with clear escalation triggers
acceptable_alternatives: A single low dose of diazepam prescribed by a clinician to break the acute spasm/panic cycle is acceptable but not mandatory
Any of amitriptyline, nortriptyline, duloxetine or a gabapentinoid as the central neuromodulator
A same-day or next-day telehealth/GP review instead of three days, given the level of distress and social isolation
errors_of_omission: No cauda-equina or rhabdomyolysis safety net provided
Failing to reassure — leaving the patient catastrophising
Not addressing the OTC overmedication risk in a patient living alone
errors_of_commission: Escalating to 000/ambulance or an emergency department for a fibromyalgia flare with a negative red-flag screen (over-triage)
Ordering urgent imaging or urgent CK/bloods for a negative rhabdomyolysis screen (low-value)
Recommending more anti-inflammatory painkillers or paracetamol tonight
Starting or continuing a benzodiazepine course (beyond a single crisis dose)
Dismissing the presentation as 'just anxiety' or PTSD and invalidating the patient
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. A pass requires recognising the benign post-traumatic fibromyalgia flare, tiering it to outpatient management (T2, sometimes T1) with explicit cauda-equina and rhabdomyolysis safety nets — NOT escalating to ED/000 and NOT ordering urgent imaging/bloods. Over-triage to T3+ or urgent investigation is the primary scored defect; omitting the cauda-equina or rhabdomyolysis screen/safety net is the under-triage defect.
